paragraphs.body: The Inevitable Relationship Between AI Scientists and Ethics Lately, there has been a brand new “automated research scientist Carl” published by the Autoscience Institute through the website AINEWS. The introduction of the new technology approach is a new achievement in AI-based science, showing that artificial intelligence (AI) has developed the ability to write peer-reviewed papers. Although the launch of the novel autonomous AI researcher advanced humans’ exploration of the technology field, it sparked the ethical issues of AI in the domain of writing. In addition, Wiwanitmkit and Wiwanitkit (2024) mentioned that the “legitimacy and transparency of AI-generated content in scientific writing is one ethical concern.” It could be seen that the rising development of AI scientists would have a significant impact on the field of academic papers.

annotations.comment: Please add a title page before the introduction, follow the style guide of the APA 7th handout, and use double spacing to format the paper. I would also add headings to organize the paper.

paragraphs.body: While adding artificial intelligence to the scientific research field, AI-generated text was considered the main reason for raised concerns in science policy. Many experts worry that as AI continues to evolve, it could blur the lines between human-created knowledge and machine-produced content. To better understand how people feel about these changes, a survey was conducted to gather opinions from general college students on the ethical issues related to AI. One key question asked was, “Do you agree with the statement that research papers could be generated by Large Language Models (LLMs)?” Interestingly, more than half of the participants agreed with this statement. This result shows that a large number of students are not only aware of AI’s growing role in the academic world but are also open to the idea of the normalization of the AI-generated research field. At the same time, this shows that the way younger people think about writing and who gets credit for it is starting to change. Some people still worry that AI might take over jobs that human researchers usually do, but many are starting to accept that AI is becoming a normal part of writing and publishing. This might be based on the points that AI tools are fast and easy to use, especially for things like making drafts, fixing grammar, or explaining hard ideas more simply. A study performed by Hao et al. (2024) shows that the number of papers published by individual scientists was 67.37 percent more while utilizing AI for support. Moreover, they get the team leader position 4 years sooner than others. The phenomenon has a high chance of lowering the engagement of in-depth research for scientists, which furthermore reduces the growth of data. They also found out from their study that “AI research spawns 24.40% less follow-on engagement, suggesting that AI papers tend to concentrate on extending the original papers, rather than forming dense interactions among each othe,r” while analysing the likeness and frequency of the citing between the AI-generated content and the original paper. Additionally, the flattening of collective knowledge proves that “AI in science has become more concentrated around specific hot topics,” leading to “overlapping ideas and redundant innovations.” If the AI-based paper is less likely to bring up interactions of real research, humans will end up suffering from shallow and fragmented research that lacks the depth of knowledge and innovation.

annotations.comment: Did you conduct the survey? If so, please clarify your role in the paper.
It would be better if the research question is open-ended; for instance, to what extent do you agree with...?

annotations.comment: Please add page numbers after direct quotes.

paragraphs.body: Although I concede that improvements in efficiency and productivity could be seen through the support of AI tools, I still maintain my position because the accuracy of AI-generated content is doubtful. A study was conducted by Khlaif et al. (2023), breaking out the potential concerns of using ChatGPT to generate scientific research. Via the prompts provided, ChatGPT came out with a research paper containing references. Throughout the study, it was discovered that the quality was unstable when comparing the different versions of paper generated by the AI. They conclude that it is weak in “developing studies”. Moreover, they found out that the percentage of references that were accessible on Google Scholar was as low as 8 percent. Hence, people should be more aware while working with AI for all writing. Without proper fact-checking and human oversight, there is a risk of spreading false or misleading information, especially in fields where accuracy is crucial.

On the other hand, it turned out that numerous people use AI as a partner to peer review their articles or papers. These tools can work like writing partners, helping to check grammar, fix awkward phrases, or make professional language easier to understand. To learn more about how people feel about this, another survey question was given out to investigate the percentage of two debating perspectives. The participants were asked to choose between the perspectives of “AI is supportive of reducing misunderstandings and hard languages” and “AI still has potential errors and biases.” Of course, it is valid to say that AI tools can support to reduce the misunderstandings of professional words or hard languages of the paper (Doskaliuk et al., 2025). However, eighty percent of the respondents agree most with the latter statement. The data demonstrates that, other than enjoying the convenience of AI tools, the users are still aware of the problems that technology might bring to the future world. This result shows that even though people enjoy the speed and support AI offers, they are also aware that it is far from perfect. AI-generated content can still include mistakes, misunderstand important details, or show bias depending on how it was trained. With the risk of existing errors and biases, the generated paper is essential to be checked by humans, leading to more workload. More and more regulations and cautions were posted. In order to maintain and ensure the “quality and credibility of public research”, editors were told to evaluate the use of AI due to the potential and possibility of including “bias and error from the chatbot”(Wiwanitmkit & Wiwanitkit, 2024). To protect academic standards, it’s important that clear rules are followed when using AI in any part of the research and writing process.

annotations.comment: I would suggest clarifying how many people viewed AI as a partner

paragraphs.body: Another interesting question is, “Can AI be listed as an author?” The investigation asks for the participants’ opinion on AI’s qualification for being listed as an author. A large percentage of people disagreed with the idea, showing that many still believe authorship should be reserved for humans. One of the main reasons behind this disagreement is that AI systems do not create truly original ideas. Instead, they work by collecting and reorganizing information that already exists. Because of this, people argue that AI-generated papers lack true innovation and personal contribution, which are essential parts of authorship. To better understand this issue, it’s important to look at the official standards for authorship. Chetwynd (2024) explains that the International Committee of Medical Journal Editors has set four key criteria that a person must meet to be considered an author. Those criteria are “Substantial contributions to the conception or design of the work; or the acquisition, analysis, or interpretation of data for the work”, “Drafting the work or reviewing it critically for important intellectual content”, “Final approval of the version to be published”, and “To be accountable for all aspects of the work in ensuring that questions related to the accuracy or integrity of any part of the work are appropriately investigated and resolved.” These criteria highlight the importance of deep thinking, decision-making, and accountability, which are things that AI cannot do on its own. Since AI lacks human judgment, creativity, and the ability to take responsibility for errors, it does not meet these standards. It cannot make conscious decisions or respond to questions about the accuracy of the work it helped generate. Therefore, while AI may assist in the writing process or help organize information, it should not be treated as a true author. Human researchers must remain fully responsible for checking the accuracy of AI-generated content and ensuring the paper meets ethical and academic standards. In order to maintain the quality and trustworthiness of academic work, AI should be treated as an assistant, not a replacement for human researchers. Thus, giving AI authorship credit could confuse the meaning of authorship itself and take away from the value of human thought, effort, and creativity in academic work.

In conclusion, while the integration of AI into scientific research and writing brings undeniable advantages in productivity and efficiency, it also raises significant ethical, practical, and intellectual concerns. The emergence of automated systems like “Carl” shows that AI has the potential to help with academic work in new and exciting ways. However, if we rely too much on AI to create research papers, it could harm the quality of the work. Research might become less original, less detailed, and less trustworthy. Many people, including students and experts, are still unsure about fully trusting AI since it can potentially make mistakes, show bias, and doesn’t truly understand what it is writing about. Some also question whether AI should be treated like a real author, since it doesn’t come up with its own new ideas or take full responsibility for the work. Due to these concerns, being careful while using AI in research or papers is essential. AI can definitely be a helpful tool, but it should not replace human thinking and creativity. To make sure research stays honest and meaningful, people should use AI responsibly and follow clear rules about its utilization.

References Chetwynd, E. (2024). Ethical use of Artificial Intelligence for scientific writing: Current trends.
